quiz_questions.question_text: The countercurrent multiplier system operates in which part of the nephron?

options.text: Proximal convoluted tubule
Distal convoluted tubule
Loop of Henle (descending and ascending limbs)
Collecting duct only

quiz_questions.explanation: The countercurrent multiplier operates in the loop of Henle, where the opposing flow directions of the descending limb (permeable to water) and ascending limb (pumps out NaCl) work together to establish the medullary osmotic gradient essential for producing concentrated urine.

quiz_questions.question_text: The thick ascending limb actively pumps out NaCl via which transporter?

options.text: SGLT2 (sodium-glucose cotransporter)
Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter (NKCC2)
Aquaporin-2 water channels
Na+/H+ exchanger

quiz_questions.explanation: The thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle uses the Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter (NKCC2) on its apical membrane to actively pump NaCl out of the tubular fluid into the medullary interstitium while remaining impermeable to water, which dilutes the tubular fluid and concentrates the medulla.

quiz_questions.question_text: Loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide) block which transporter and in which nephron segment?

options.text: SGLT2 in the PCT
Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle
Aquaporin channels in the collecting duct
Na+/K+ ATPase in all nephron segments

quiz_questions.explanation: Loop diuretics such as furosemide (Lasix) specifically inhibit the Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter in the thick ascending limb, blocking NaCl reabsorption and disrupting the medullary concentration gradient. This causes massive excretion of sodium, potassium, chloride, and water, making loop diuretics the most potent class of diuretics.

quiz_questions.question_text: ADH acts on which nephron segment to increase water reabsorption?

options.text: Proximal convoluted tubule
Loop of Henle
Distal convoluted tubule
Collecting duct, inserting aquaporin-2 channels

quiz_questions.explanation: ADH (vasopressin) primarily acts on the principal cells of the collecting duct by binding to V2 receptors, which triggers the insertion of aquaporin-2 water channels into the apical membrane. This allows water to move from the tubular fluid into the hypertonic medullary interstitium, concentrating the urine.

quiz_questions.question_text: Without ADH, urine becomes:

options.text: Very concentrated and low in volume
Dilute and high in volume (as seen in diabetes insipidus)
Normal in concentration and volume
Completely absent (anuria)

quiz_questions.explanation: Without ADH, the collecting duct remains impermeable to water, so the dilute tubular fluid leaving the ascending limb passes through without water being reabsorbed. This results in large volumes (up to 15-20 L/day) of very dilute urine, as seen in diabetes insipidus.

quiz_questions.question_text: Aldosterone promotes sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion in the:

options.text: Proximal convoluted tubule only
Loop of Henle only
DCT and collecting duct principal cells
Glomerulus

quiz_questions.explanation: Aldosterone is a mineralocorticoid that acts on principal cells of the late DCT and collecting duct by upregulating epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) and Na+/K+ ATPase pumps, increasing Na+ reabsorption and K+ secretion. Water follows sodium osmotically, increasing blood volume and pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: Inulin clearance is the gold standard for measuring GFR because inulin is:

options.text: Actively secreted by the tubules but not filtered
Freely filtered at the glomerulus and neither reabsorbed nor secreted by the tubules
Completely reabsorbed in the PCT
Produced by the kidney itself

quiz_questions.explanation: Inulin is an exogenous polysaccharide that is freely filtered at the glomerulus and is neither reabsorbed nor secreted by the renal tubules. This means its clearance rate exactly equals the GFR, making it the gold standard reference substance for measuring glomerular filtration rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: ANP (atrial natriuretic peptide) is released when:

options.text: Blood pressure is dangerously low
Atrial walls are stretched due to volume overload
Potassium levels are critically low
ADH levels are maximally elevated

quiz_questions.explanation: ANP is released by atrial cardiomyocytes when the atrial walls are stretched by increased blood volume (volume overload). It acts as a natural counter-regulatory hormone to RAAS by promoting sodium and water excretion, inhibiting renin and aldosterone, and causing vasodilation to reduce blood volume and pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: PTH affects the kidney by promoting:

options.text: Calcium excretion and phosphate retention
Calcium reabsorption in the DCT and activation of vitamin D (calcitriol)
Sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion
Bicarbonate excretion and acid retention

quiz_questions.explanation: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) acts on the kidney in three important ways: it increases calcium reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule, promotes phosphate excretion (phosphaturia), and stimulates the enzyme 1-alpha-hydroxylase to convert 25-hydroxyvitamin D to its active form (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D/calcitriol), which enhances intestinal calcium absorption.

quiz_questions.question_text: The correct sequence of urine formation processes is:

options.text: Secretion, filtration, reabsorption, excretion
Filtration, reabsorption, secretion, excretion
Excretion, filtration, reabsorption, secretion
Reabsorption, filtration, excretion, secretion

quiz_questions.explanation: Urine formation follows four sequential processes: (1) glomerular filtration creates the initial filtrate, (2) tubular reabsorption recovers useful substances back into the blood, (3) tubular secretion adds additional waste substances from the blood into the tubular fluid, and (4) excretion eliminates the final urine from the body.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is PAH (para-aminohippuric acid) clearance used to estimate?

options.text: Glomerular filtration rate (GFR)
Renal plasma flow (RPF), since PAH is both filtered and secreted so that approximately 90% is cleared in a single pass
Total blood volume
Cardiac output

quiz_questions.explanation: PAH clearance estimates effective renal plasma flow (ERPF) because PAH is both freely filtered at the glomerulus AND actively secreted by the proximal tubule, so approximately 90% of PAH is removed from plasma in a single pass through the kidney. This makes PAH clearance the best clinical estimate of renal blood flow.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term filtration fraction mean?

options.text: The percentage of cardiac output that goes to the kidneys
The fraction of renal plasma flow that is filtered at the glomerulus, calculated as GFR/RPF (normally about 20%)
The percentage of filtrate that becomes urine
The ratio of sodium to water in the final urine

quiz_questions.explanation: The filtration fraction (FF) is the proportion of renal plasma flow that is actually filtered across the glomerular capillaries, calculated as GFR divided by RPF. Normally about 20% (125 mL/min GFR / 625 mL/min RPF = 0.20), it reflects the efficiency of glomerular filtration relative to renal blood flow.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the transport maximum (Tm)?

options.text: The maximum blood pressure the kidneys can autoregulate
The maximum rate at which a substance can be reabsorbed or secreted by the renal tubules when all carrier proteins are saturated
The maximum volume of urine the bladder can hold
The maximum concentration of the medullary interstitium

quiz_questions.explanation: Transport maximum (Tm) is the maximum rate at which a substance can be transported across the tubular epithelium, limited by the number and capacity of carrier proteins. When plasma concentrations exceed the Tm (as with glucose in diabetes), the excess substance cannot be reabsorbed and appears in the urine.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term free water clearance mean?

options.text: The total volume of water filtered by the glomerulus per day
A measure of the kidney's ability to excrete or retain water relative to solute, indicating whether the urine is dilute or concentrated
The amount of water consumed by the patient per day
The rate at which IV fluids are administered during rehydration

quiz_questions.explanation: Free water clearance (CH2O) quantifies the volume of solute-free water the kidneys excrete or retain per unit time. A positive free water clearance indicates dilute urine (excess water excretion), while a negative value indicates concentrated urine (water retention), reflecting the kidney's ability to regulate body fluid osmolality.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the vasa recta?

options.text: The blood vessels supplying the renal cortex only
Specialized peritubular capillaries that run parallel to the loops of Henle in the medulla, functioning as countercurrent exchangers to preserve the medullary osmotic gradient
The afferent and efferent arterioles of the glomerulus
The venous drainage system of the renal pelvis

quiz_questions.explanation: The vasa recta are hairpin-shaped capillaries that descend into the medulla alongside the loops of Henle of juxtamedullary nephrons. They function as countercurrent exchangers, allowing blood to supply the medulla with oxygen and nutrients while preserving the medullary osmotic gradient essential for urine concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does BUN (blood urea nitrogen) measure?

options.text: The amount of creatinine produced by muscle metabolism
The nitrogen content of urea in the blood, which rises in renal failure, dehydration, high protein intake, and GI bleeding
The level of ammonia in the urine
The blood concentration of uric acid from purine metabolism

quiz_questions.explanation: BUN measures the amount of urea nitrogen in the blood. Urea is a waste product of protein metabolism produced by the liver. BUN rises in renal failure (decreased excretion), dehydration (hemoconcentration), high protein diets, GI bleeding (blood protein digestion), and catabolic states, making it a useful but non-specific marker of kidney function.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the BUN-to-creatinine ratio used to assess?

options.text: Liver function and bile production
Whether azotemia (elevated BUN/creatinine) is prerenal, intrinsic renal, or postrenal in origin
The severity of proteinuria
The adequacy of dialysis treatment

quiz_questions.explanation: The BUN-to-creatinine ratio helps differentiate the cause of kidney dysfunction. A ratio greater than 20:1 suggests prerenal azotemia (dehydration, heart failure) where urea is preferentially reabsorbed. A normal ratio of 10-20:1 with elevated values suggests intrinsic renal disease, and the clinical context helps identify postrenal causes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does proteinuria indicate?

options.text: Normal kidney function with excess dietary protein
Damage to the glomerular filtration barrier, allowing proteins to leak into the urine
Excess protein reabsorption in the proximal convoluted tubule
Active secretion of protein by the distal tubule

quiz_questions.explanation: Proteinuria (protein in the urine, especially albuminuria) is a hallmark sign of glomerular damage, as healthy glomeruli prevent large proteins from entering the filtrate. It is an important early marker of diabetic nephropathy, hypertensive kidney disease, and glomerulonephritis, and its presence and degree help guide diagnosis and treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal range for urine specific gravity?

options.text: 1.000 (same as pure water)

quiz_questions.explanation: Normal urine specific gravity ranges from 1.001 to 1.035, reflecting the concentration of dissolved solutes relative to pure water (1.000). A low specific gravity indicates dilute urine (as in diabetes insipidus or excessive fluid intake), while a high specific gravity indicates concentrated urine (as in dehydration or SIADH).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the tubuloglomerular feedback (TGF) mechanism?

options.text: A mechanism by which the collecting duct signals the pituitary to release ADH
A local feedback mechanism where the macula densa senses NaCl concentration in the tubular fluid and adjusts afferent arteriole tone to regulate GFR
A systemic feedback loop involving the RAAS pathway only
The process by which the PCT communicates with the loop of Henle

quiz_questions.explanation: Tubuloglomerular feedback is a local autoregulatory mechanism in which the macula densa cells of the JGA sense NaCl concentration in the tubular fluid at the DCT. When NaCl is high (indicating high GFR), the macula densa signals the afferent arteriole to constrict, reducing GFR. When NaCl is low, the arteriole dilates to increase GFR, maintaining stable filtration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with severe congestive heart failure is prescribed furosemide (a loop diuretic) but develops hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis as side effects. A physiology student is asked to explain the mechanism. Why does blocking the Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter lead to potassium loss and alkalosis?

options.text: Furosemide directly destroys potassium ions in the tubular fluid
Increased sodium delivery to the collecting duct drives more Na+/K+ exchange via aldosterone, increasing potassium secretion, and volume contraction stimulates H+ secretion
Furosemide blocks potassium reabsorption in the PCT specifically
Loop diuretics increase ADH secretion, which directly causes potassium loss

quiz_questions.explanation: By blocking NaCl reabsorption in the thick ascending limb, furosemide increases sodium delivery to the collecting duct. Here, aldosterone-stimulated principal cells reabsorb the excess sodium and secrete more potassium in exchange, causing hypokalemia. The volume contraction also activates RAAS and stimulates H+ secretion in the collecting duct, leading to metabolic alkalosis (contraction alkalosis).

quiz_questions.question_text: During an AP exam, a student is presented with the following scenario: A substance X has a clearance higher than inulin clearance. What can be concluded about how substance X is handled by the kidney?

options.text: Substance X is only filtered and not reabsorbed or secreted
Substance X is filtered AND actively secreted by the tubules, adding more to the urine than filtration alone
Substance X is filtered but partially reabsorbed by the tubules
Substance X is not filtered at the glomerulus at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Since inulin clearance equals GFR (inulin is freely filtered but neither reabsorbed nor secreted), a substance with clearance greater than inulin must undergo net tubular secretion in addition to filtration. This means the tubules are actively adding the substance from peritubular blood into the tubular fluid, increasing its excretion rate beyond what filtration alone would produce (e.g., PAH, penicillin).

quiz_questions.question_text: A substance Y has a clearance less than inulin clearance but greater than zero. What does this tell you about how substance Y is handled by the nephron?

options.text: Substance Y is filtered AND secreted
Substance Y is filtered at the glomerulus but partially reabsorbed by the tubules
Substance Y is not filtered and is only secreted
Substance Y is completely reabsorbed and none appears in the urine

quiz_questions.explanation: A clearance less than inulin but greater than zero indicates that the substance is freely filtered at the glomerulus but then partially reabsorbed by the tubular epithelium. This net reabsorption removes some of the substance from the urine, resulting in a lower clearance than would be expected from filtration alone (e.g., urea, sodium).

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient presents with the following arterial blood gas: pH 7.28, pCO2 25 mmHg, HCO3- 12 mEq/L. On an AP exam, you are asked to identify the primary disorder and the compensation. What is your analysis?

options.text: Respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation
Metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3-) with respiratory compensation (low pCO2 from hyperventilation)
Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation
Metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation

quiz_questions.explanation: The low pH (7.28) confirms acidemia. The low HCO3- (12 mEq/L, normal 22-26) indicates the primary problem is metabolic acidosis (loss of bicarbonate or gain of acid). The low pCO2 (25 mmHg, normal 35-45) represents respiratory compensation through hyperventilation, as the lungs attempt to blow off CO2 to raise the pH toward normal.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physiology professor asks: If you constrict the efferent arteriole of a nephron, what happens to GFR, filtration fraction, and peritubular capillary oncotic pressure? Assume no change in renal plasma flow initially.

options.text: GFR decreases, filtration fraction decreases, peritubular oncotic pressure decreases
GFR increases, filtration fraction increases, peritubular capillary oncotic pressure increases
GFR stays the same, filtration fraction decreases, peritubular oncotic pressure increases
GFR decreases, filtration fraction stays the same, peritubular oncotic pressure decreases

quiz_questions.explanation: Efferent arteriole constriction increases glomerular hydrostatic pressure (by restricting outflow), which increases GFR. Since more plasma is filtered relative to flow, the filtration fraction (GFR/RPF) increases. The blood leaving the glomerulus is more concentrated (more protein-rich), so the peritubular capillary oncotic pressure increases, which actually enhances proximal tubule reabsorption.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion) is retaining excess water. On an AP exam, you are asked to predict the changes in serum sodium, urine osmolality, and urine volume. What would you expect?

options.text: High serum sodium, dilute urine, high urine volume
Low serum sodium (dilutional hyponatremia), concentrated urine (high osmolality), and low urine volume
Normal serum sodium, normal urine, normal volume
High serum sodium, concentrated urine, high urine volume

quiz_questions.explanation: In SIADH, excess ADH causes inappropriate water reabsorption in the collecting duct, producing inappropriately concentrated urine (high osmolality) with low volume. The retained water dilutes the serum sodium, causing hyponatremia. This is a classic AP exam topic requiring understanding of ADH physiology and its pathological excess.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is studying a patient with Conn's syndrome (primary hyperaldosteronism from an adrenal adenoma). What changes in serum sodium, potassium, blood pressure, and pH would you predict?

options.text: Low sodium, high potassium, low blood pressure, acidosis
High sodium (mild hypernatremia), low potassium (hypokalemia), hypertension, and metabolic alkalosis
Normal sodium, normal potassium, hypotension, and normal pH
Low sodium, low potassium, normal blood pressure, and respiratory acidosis

quiz_questions.explanation: Excess aldosterone causes increased Na+ reabsorption (mild hypernatremia and water retention leading to hypertension), increased K+ secretion (hypokalemia), and increased H+ secretion in the collecting duct (metabolic alkalosis). This clinical constellation of hypertension, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis is the classic triad of primary hyperaldosteronism.

quiz_questions.question_text: During an AP exam review, a student is asked: Why is plasma creatinine a better clinical marker of GFR than BUN, even though both are elevated in kidney failure?

options.text: Creatinine is produced at a constant rate from muscle metabolism and is not significantly affected by diet or hydration, while BUN varies with protein intake, hydration status, and other non-renal factors
BUN is always normal in kidney disease, while creatinine always rises
Creatinine is only produced when GFR decreases, making it a direct indicator
BUN is measured in the urine while creatinine is measured in the blood

quiz_questions.explanation: Creatinine is produced at a relatively constant rate from muscle creatine phosphate metabolism and is freely filtered without significant reabsorption, making its plasma concentration inversely proportional to GFR. BUN, while also useful, is influenced by many non-renal factors including dietary protein intake, GI bleeding, dehydration, liver function, and catabolic states, making it less specific for kidney function.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient receiving lithium therapy for bipolar disorder develops polyuria (excessive urine output) and polydipsia (excessive thirst). Lab values show dilute urine with low osmolality despite elevated serum osmolality. ADH levels are elevated. What type of diabetes insipidus does this represent?

options.text: Central diabetes insipidus from inadequate ADH production
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, where the collecting duct is resistant to ADH despite adequate ADH levels
Psychogenic polydipsia from excessive water drinking
SIADH causing water retention

quiz_questions.explanation: Lithium is a well-known cause of nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, where it impairs the collecting duct's ability to respond to ADH by interfering with aquaporin-2 channel insertion. ADH levels are elevated (the pituitary is appropriately responding to dehydration), but the kidneys cannot concentrate the urine, resulting in large volumes of dilute urine despite high serum osmolality.

quiz_questions.question_text: On an AP exam, a student is presented with the following scenario: A patient has glycosuria with a normal blood glucose of 85 mg/dL. The student must explain this finding. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Undiagnosed diabetes mellitus causing elevated blood glucose
A defect in the proximal tubule glucose transporters (renal glucosuria), resulting in inability to reabsorb glucose despite normal blood levels
A laboratory error in the glucose measurement
Excess ADH causing glucose to be concentrated in the urine

quiz_questions.explanation: Glycosuria with normal blood glucose levels indicates a defect in the proximal tubule's glucose reabsorption machinery (renal glucosuria). This can be caused by mutations in the SGLT2 transporter, reducing the transport maximum for glucose so that even normal filtered glucose loads exceed the tubule's diminished reabsorptive capacity. This is distinct from diabetic glucosuria, where the glucose load simply exceeds normal transport capacity.
